coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots/polyaniline (CQDs/PANI) nanocomposite was successfully prepared by in-situ polymerization of aniline. CQDs were synthesized hydrothermally from gelatin with a diameter size of 4.2 nm and a 17% quantum yield. FTIR, UV–vis absorption, fluorescence spectrophotometer, XRD, TEM, XPS and lifetime decay were used to characterize the obtained nanocomposite. The formation of PANI revealed a high quenching effect on CQDs where the TEM images showed that the formed CQDs were greatly embedded in PANI matrix. In this study, CQDs/PANI nanocomposite was used for the detection of picric acid (PA) in the range 0.37–1.42 μM with a low detection limit (LOD) of 0.056 μM. The prepared sensor showed good enhancement and sensitivity towards PA in comparison to pristine CQDs and other nanostructured materials. The mechanism of PA detection has been studied where it was observed that PA is electrostatically interacted to the nanocomposite through – OH group of PA and the protonated PANI salt formed in CQDs/PANI nanocomposite by fluorescence resonance energy transfer applications. The proposed CQDs/PANI sensor was then utilized in real water samples and successfully determined the different amounts of PA spiked into tap water.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots/polyaniline (CQDs/PANI) nanocomposite was successfully prepared by in-situ polymerization of aniline. CQDs were synthesized hydrothermally from gelatin with a diameter size of 4.2 nm and a 17% quantum yield. FTIR, UV–vis absorption, fluorescence spectrophotometer, XRD, TEM, XPS and lifetime decay were used to characterize the obtained nanocomposite. The formation of PANI revealed a high quenching effect on CQDs where the TEM images showed that the formed CQDs were greatly embedded in PANI matrix. In this study, CQDs/PANI nanocomposite was used for the detection of picric acid (PA) in the range 0.37–1.42 μM with a low detection limit (LOD) of 0.056 μM. The prepared sensor showed good enhancement and sensitivity towards PA in comparison to pristine CQDs and other nanostructured materials. The mechanism of PA detection has been studied where it was observed that PA is electrostatically interacted to the nanocomposite through – OH group of PA and the protonated PANI salt formed in CQDs/PANI nanocomposite by fluorescence resonance energy transfer applications. The proposed CQDs/PANI sensor was then utilized in real water samples and successfully determined the different amounts of PA spiked into tap water.

ce:section.ce:para: The nanomaterials have captured the researchers’ attention since the start of the 21st century [1,2] due its great potential in applications such as drug delivery [3], electronics manufacture [4], environmental protection [5], and energy storage [6]. This can be attributed to their unique properties resulting in different composition of size and shape in the nanoscale that entered people’s daily life gradually [7,8]. Compared to bulk materials, they feature different physical and chemical properties making them highly reactive. The use of nanocomposites incorporating the dual advantage of nanoparticles and polymers exhibit an amazing optical, mechanical, and electrical properties. Therefore, it is important to study such nanocomposite materials having different nanoparticle size and shape [9].

Conducting polymers (CPs) have attracted high interest since the discovery of the polyacetylene conductivity in 1977 owing to their large potential for electroluminescence applications and amazing physical properties [10]. Among these conductive polymers are polypyrrole (PPY), polythiophene (PTH), and polyaniline (PANI). PANI is considered as a unique and promising polymer [9,11–13] due to the nitrogen heteroatoms existence formed by alternating benzenoid and quinoid groups along the backbone and, hence, was chosen in the current research work. Owing to its environmental and chemical stability, high electric conductibility, non-toxicity, high redox properties, optical properties, and simple preparation [9,14,15], PANI is found to focus on many applications in supercapacitors [16], corrosion protection [17], light emitting diodes [18],catalysts [19,20], efficient adsorbents [21,22], and sensors [23]. The formed nanostructural CPs received a high attention due to their different properties attained from those of the coarse structures that make them an interesting material in sensors field [24].

Polyaniline-based nanocomposites have been recently used as sensing materials with carbon nanomaterials providing a higher sensitivity. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are known to be rising stars of fluorescent carbonaceous nanoparticles with an sp2 conjugated core and a spherical structure of size diameter less than 10 nm [25–28]. They have emerged as excellent fluorophores [29] that possess functional groups mostly of hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxylic groups on their surface which spark off their high hydrophilicity and ability to interact with other polymeric species [30]. CQDs received much acknowledgement due to their unique properties as low cytotoxicity, good stability, ease of preparation, wavelength excitation dependence, biocompatibility, and high surface area [25,26,31,32]. Their properties lead to promising applications in the fields of optical imaging [33] , optoelectronics [34], catalysis [35], medical diagnosis [36], drug delivery [37], light-emitting diodes [38], and sensing [39,40]. Recently, they have been developed through the use of natural precursors like citric acid [41], coffee grounds [42], orange juice [43], silkworm [44], honey [45], etc. However, their preparation still need to be obtained through an easy and environment-friendly method like hydrothermal technique which is one of the most simple methods for their cheap apparatus, simple manipulation, and single step preparation [46]. Besides, CQDs are known to be good energy donors than other fluorescent materials in fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) applications [47]. Furthermore, the quantum effect and emissive traps are the main factors responsible for the fluorescence of CQDs which then influence their photoluminescence (PL) properties. But still, the modifications applied to CQDs can produce more defects that trigger further on their fluorescence [48].

As a result, the formation of CQDs/PANI nanocomposite is obtained in current research work, where PANI is a polymer with a planar π-π electronic conjugated system and could be used as FRET acceptor with CQDs providing a high efficiency in quenching the CQDs emission [15]. This allowed the formed nanocomposite to be used as a sensor probe for the detection of nitroexplosive compounds as 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT), picric acid (PA), and 2,4-dinitrotoluene (DNT) [11,49,50]. These explosive materials have been used because they are simple, easy to deploy, and cause enormous damage due to the presence of reactive nitrate compounds that are responsible for a large potential energy accompanied by low vapor pressures at room temperature, illumination, sound, and heat [49,50]. They are commonly used in pesticides, pharmaceuticals, and dye industry. On the other hand, these applications are not much desirable because they are the main source of industrial wastes destroying the surrounding environment [51,52]. It is noteworthy that PA is considered to be a more powerful explosive than TNT. It was used until World War I as an explosive material [53,54]. The ease of solubility of PA in aqueous medium with its electron deficient property makes its degradation to be difficult thus, forming a high contamination in soil, groundwater and also evokes the health disorders like cancer, anaemia, skin/eye irritation, and destruction of respiratory organs [55,56]. According to these circumstances, some techniques were applied such as spectrophotometry [57], gas chromatography (GC) [58], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [59], and fluorescence [60]. However, most of these techniques are expensive, complicated, and time consuming thus limiting their applications [56,61]. Hence, fluorescence signaling is greatly applied as a probe for PA detection because of its high sensitivity and fast response in solution that become challenging in the present time [50,56,62]. Herein, we report a synthesis of CQDs/PANI nanocomposite by in-situ polymerization of aniline using ammonium persulfate as an oxidant. CQDs were synthesized hydrothermally from gelatin (porcine skin Type A) with quantum yield 17%. Results showed an enhancement in sensitivity towards PA in comparison with pristine CQDs at very low concentrations using fluorescence quenching based sensor.

Gelatin (porcine skin Type A) obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, aniline (El-Gomhouria, Egypt) was purified by vacuum distillation. Ammonium persulfate (APS) from Sigma-Aldrich, hydrochloric acid (HCl) with purity 37% and picric acid (PA) were obtained from Fisher Scientific UK and used without further purification. Aromatic and nitro compounds were used for further tests like ammonium nitrate, benzoic acid (BA), benzene (B), P-nitrophenol (PNP), resorcinol (Res), 3-aminophenol (3-AP), 4-aminophenol (4-AP), toluene (Tol), nitrobenzene (NB), and o-chlorophenol (o-ClP). Metal ions as: Co2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Pd2+, Pt4+, Ca2+, Cu2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Sn2+, and Zn2+ were also used for selectivity tests.

Carbon quantum dots were synthesized as described before but with small modifications [46]. 0.8 gm of gelatin was dissolved in distilled water at 50–60 °C. The mixture was then poured into a stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 3 h. Finally, the reactor was left to cool at room temperature. The light yellow solution was centrifuged at 14,800 rpm for 30 min to remove the agglomerated particles.

Different concentrations of aniline (An) were prepared in 0.1 M HCl aqueous solution forming anilinium salt solution. 3 mL of CQDs colloidal suspension (10 mg/mL) was then added to anilinium solutions and mixed for 10 min at room temperature. Afterwards, the mixture was heated at 80 °C for 20 min. The mixture was then left to cool at room temperature and put in ice bath till reaching a temperature below <5 °C. A specific amount of APS was added to the mixture with a molar ratio of 1:1 mmol to aniline and the reaction was left undisturbed for 30 min till polymerization. The solution was then polymeized slowly forming PANI emeraldine salt and the color of solution turned green. The final product was then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min and washed 3 times with HCl solution till the solution becomes clear.

Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were accomplished by Bruker VERTEX 70v connected to platinum ATR model V-100 in the wave number (400–4000 cm−1). UV–vis absorption (Hitachi U-3900) in a wavelength range 190–900 nm was applied. The fluorescence measurements were carried out by Hitachi F-2700 with light source Xenon lamp (Xe) of 150 W. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were examined under JEOL JEM-2100F. X-ray diffractometry (XRD) was obtained using Shimadzu XRD-6100 with maximum output 3 kW of copper source to determine the crystal structure of the material. The fluorescence decay was recorded by fluorescence lifetime measuring system Horiba FluoroCube 3000U-EYM1. The laser source was set at 372 nm. The zeta potential (ζ) of CQDs and nanocomposite were determined using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano Series using standard laser at 10 mW operating at k = 633 nm. The (ζ) measurements were accomplished using deionized H2O as the dispersion medium. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was collected on Kα (Thermo Fisher Scientific) with monochromatic X-ray radiation −10 to 1350 eV spot size 400 μm at pressure 10–9 mbar with full spectrum pass energy 200 eV.

Quantum yield of CQDs was achieved by using quinine sulfate (QS) as a standard reference (QY = 0.54) in 0.1 M H2SO4 at excitation wavelength 360 nm. The absorbance of CQDs and quinine sulfate solutions were kept below 0.1. The quantum yield measurements were calculated by Eq. (1).
(1)

where QYs , QYref are the quantum yields of CQDs and quinine sulfate respectively, I represents the fluorescence intensity curves, and η is the solvent refractive index used for both which is water (η = 1.33). It was found that the QY of the formed CQDs reached 17% (Fig. S1). Furthermore, the QY of the prepared nanocomposite was also measured and found to be 13%.

Aqueous solutions of PA with different concentrations were readily prepared in the range of 0.37–4.28 μM, mixed with CQDs colloidal suspension (250 μL), and the fluorescence intensity was then measured after 1 min. The same procedure was followed for checking the detection affinity of CQDs/PANI nanocomposite towards PA. The fluorescence showed an increase in quenching behavior with the continuous addition of PA concentration showing a linear relationship with a high sensitivity towards PA as described by Stern-Volmer equation (2):
(2)

where I ₀ and I are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of PA respectively, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching sensitivity and [Q] is the concentration of PA.
The sensitive and selective study of PA was further studied in the presence of other nitro and aromatic compounds, and different metal ions with a concentration of 4 μM as will be discussed later.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of PA in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Picric acid was spiked at different concentrations in tap water with colloidal suspension of CQDs/PANI nanocomposite (250 μL) and the data was recorded at room temperature.

The morphology of the CQDs/PANI material was shown in Fig. 1 . Clearly, TEM image of CQDs showed spherical shaped dots of different size with an average diameter distribution of about 4.2 nm (Fig. 1a). Furthermore, the crystallite lattice was also measured and found to be around 0.2 nm that corresponds to (100) plane. The formation of the CQDs/PANI nanocomposite was further confirmed by the High Resolution TEM (HRTEM) images shown in Fig. 1b and c, which revealed the embedded CQDs into the PANI matrix. This is might be due to the surface modifications of the interaction between the functional groups located onto the surface of CQDs and PANI matrix.

The crystal structure was analyzed by powder wide-angle XRD as shown in Fig. 1d. PANI showed a broad peak at 2θ = 24.8° for (200) plane which might be attributed to its amorphous nature representing the distance between quinoid and benzenoid repetitive rings in PANI chains, that is more dominant than the diffraction pattern located at 20.1° as discussed previously in literature [63–65]. Two broad diffraction patterns located at around 2θ = 22.8° and 43.8° were related to (002) and (100) planes of CQDs, respectively. Furthermore, CQDs/PANI nanocomposite exhibits the diffraction patterns of 2θ = 20.1° and 24.8° which are slightly shifted to 2θ = 20.3° and 24.9°, indicating the distortion of the graphitic planes present in the formed CQDs that are generated due to their interaction with PANI. The interplanar spacing from XRD pattern of CQDs indicates the (002) plane is 0.39 nm which is larger than graphitic carbon (0.34) [43] that might be ascribed to the presence of abundant functional groups formed onto the surface of CQDs, this indicates the increase in its amorphous nature and the introduction of more defect sites produced [66]. The (100) plane was calculated and found equal to 0.2 nm [46,67] according to Bragg’s equation (3) [68]:
(3)
d =
n λ
2 sin θ
where d is the interplanar spacing, n (an integer) is the “order” of reflection, λ is the wavelength of the incident X-ray (1.54 Å), and
θ
is the X-ray incident angle (rad).

The optical properties for CQDs were also studied using UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectrophotometers. Results showed the presence of a broad peak in the absorption spectrum at about 250–300 nm may be attributed to π-π* or n-π* transitions (Fig. 2 a). PANI absorption showed two characteristic peaks located at 372 and 735 nm (inset Fig. 2a), indicating the formation of PANI Emeraldine Salt (PANI-ES) due to the π-π* transition of benzenoid ring and exciton transition to the quinoid rings, respectively [15,69]. Furthermore, the CQDs/PANI nanocomposite behavior showed a peak at 250 nm related to CQDs and two peaks were shifted at 282 and 530 nm represent the formation of PANI.

A fluorescence measurement was placed in Fig. 2b and showed that CQDs are excitation dependent wavelength in the range 300–500 nm and the highest intensity was shown at an excitation wavelength 360 nm then decreased gradually as it shifts to higher wavelengths.

FTIR spectra of CQDs (Fig. 3 a) showed a peak at 3437 cm−1 which is characteristic to OH bond. The stretching vibration band of CO at 1660 cm−1, two absorption peaks at 2952 and 1400 cm−1 are related to the stretching vibrations of CH and CC which indicate the presence of alkyl and aryl groups formed in the CQDs. In case of PANI, a band appeared at 3445 cm−1 corresponds to a secondary NH amino group stretching band. Moreover, the bands located at 1569 and 1474 cm−1 are caused by the stretching vibrations of benzenoid (CC) and quinoid (CN) rings in the synthesized PANI. Peaks at 802 and 1124 cm−1 are related to aromatic NH in and out of plane-bending vibration for 1, 4-disubstituted rings formed by polymerization [9,63,70]. On the other hand, CQDs/PANI nanocomposite showed a slight shift in PANI peaks to higher frequencies, implying the interaction between PANI and functional groups located on the surface of CQDs.

For more clarification and indication of the surface composition of the CQDs and the CQDs/PANI nanocomposite, the XPS data was applied as shown in Fig. 3b–d. Wide-scan XPS spectra of CQDs showed the presence of C1s, N1s, and O1s located at 287.4, 401.3, and 533.9 eV, respectively explaining the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen elements (Fig. 3b). The high resolution for C1s (Fig. 3c) is deconvoluted into four main peaks at 284.1, 284.7, 285.9, and 287.6 eV which display the existence of CC, CC, (CN/CO) and CO bands that confirm the presence of N and O-rich functional groups on the surface of CQDs [67,71–74]. On the other hand, a shift has occurred in the CQDs/PANI nanocomposite where the binding energies at 284.4, 285.1, 286.3, and 288.5 eV were attributed to CC/CC, CN, CO, and CO bands respectively for C1s (Fig. 3d). For CQDs N1s, two prominent peaks appeared at 399.6 and 401.7 eV that were attributed to CNC and NH [40,73,75,76]. Furthermore, the CQDs O1s spectra (Fig. S2) showed three characteristic peaks at 530.8, 531.3, and 531.9 eV that characteristic for CO, COH, and COC bands [32,71,74,77]. The N1s of composite (Fig. S2) high resolution spectra also showed a noticeable shift to 400.2 and 402.2 eV that explains the presence of pyridinic-N and NO. The atomic ratios of CN peaks in both CQDs and CQDs/PANI nanocomposite were found to be 2.6 and 3.8. This indicates the higher ratio of nitrogen present in CQDs/PANI nanocomposite than pristine CQDs confirming the PANI formation in the presence of CQDs. Under such conditions, the dots were successfully embedded in the polymer matrix. These observations were agreeable with the FTIR and TEM data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of contact time on the interaction of CQDs and aniline
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of time for the interaction of aniline dissolved in 0.1 M HCl solution to form anilinium cations with CQDs solution after mixing and annealing was studied. The fluorescence emission intensity of the CQDs was measured at different time intervals after mixing at room temperature. It was observed that the fluorescence intensity decreases with time and reached stability after 10 min (Fig. S3). However, an enhancement in the association of the anilinium cation monomers with CQDs occurred after annealing the solution mixture at 80 °C for 20 min. This is indicated by decreasing the fluorescence intensity in comparison with mixing only at room temperature. That allowed a sufficient time for the aniline monomers to associate onto CQDs till reaching the saturation time.

The fluorescence measurements of CQDs/PANI nanocomposites were measured after annealing and polymerization. It was found that after annealing, the quenching increased with the increase of anilinium cations (in 0.1 M HCl solution) concentrations which might be due to the electrostatic interaction of the formed anilinium ions onto the surface of CQDs under annealing (Fig. S4). Afterwards, the APS dissolved in 0.1 M HCl solution with the same ratio of aniline concentrations was added to CQDs/An solutions. The in-situ polymerization of aniline took place where the quenching of the CQDs dramatically increased while the fluorescence intensity decreased, Fig. 4 . This could be due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) from the nonradiative excited states of CQDs to PANI π-system acting as acceptors leading to a high quenching [15,78].

Furthermore, the lifetime decay was measured at different concentrations of aniline, followed by CQDs/PANI nanocomposites at different concentrations of aniline and APS in aqueous 0.1 M HCl solution. The decay curve was fitted to a bi-exponential function by Eq. (4):
(4)
Y =

where A1, A2 describe the pre-exponential factors and τ1, τ2 represent the lifetimes. In order to discuss the quenching mechanisms whether it is dynamic or static associations, the fluorescence decay of CQDs at different concentrations of aniline were carefully studied (Fig. S5). Clearly, a decrease in fluorescence lifetime occurs after increasing the aniline concentrations from 1 to 5 mmol with CQDs, confirming that the resultant fluorescence quenching is dynamic. Since CQDs solution was added to the aniline monomers in acidic medium (0.1 M HCl), it was proposed that the anilinium cations through the NH2 + groups can conjugate with functional groups attached onto the surface of CQDs and π electron density of the CQDs. The strong interaction between anilinium cations and CQDs was supported by measuring the average lifetime at different aniline concentrations. It can be clearly seen that the average lifetimes decrease by increasing the concentration of anilinium cations from 3 to 0.86 ns. This is due to the increase of the amount of aniline monomers conjugated with the CQDs and the decrease of the electron delocalization densities of the CQDs, resulting in decreasing the average lifetimes [9]. On the other hand, a static quenching was obtained for CQDs/PANI nanocomposite after in-situ polymerization of aniline to form different PANI concentrations, as shown in Fig. S5. Accordingly, there are two processes that might occur: (1) CQDs would be capped with the formed PANI leading to stabilize the average lifetime even with increasing the concentration of aniline monomers and (2) a homopolymerization between aniline monomers might occur in the solution resulting in quenching of the fluorescence intensity. Based on these results, the produced CQDs/PANI nanocomposite at a concentration of 1.0 mmol of aniline was selected as the sensor probe for PA due to its high fluorescence efficiency compared to other aniline concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity
ce:section.ce:para: pH is a key factor to study the sensing performance of PA by CQDs and CQDs/PANI nanocomposite. Their fluorescence intensities were measured at different pH media. The pH solutions were adjusted over a wide range by NaOH (0.1 M) and HCl (0.1 M) aqueous solutions and subsequently mixed for 1 min. The fluorescence spectra were reported at excitation wavelength 360 nm. When the pH of CQDs solution was below 4, the fluorescence intensity was low and increased upon reaching pH 4. This was followed by almost constant fluorescence intensity that decreased, gently, with pH up to 10. This could be attributed to the conversion of the functional groups attached to the surface of CQDs to anions at high pH value (Fig. 5 ). At higher pH and strong basic medium, the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on CQDs surface form intermolecular hydrogen bonding, demonstrating that CQDs are pH–dependent [79]. Similar results were obtained by Zhou et al [80]. Meanwhile, the effect of pH on the CQDs/PANI nanocomposite fluorescence intensity was also studied. Hence, the fluorescence intensity was influenced by the solution pH that greatly affects the conjugation between CQDs and PANI. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs/PANI nanocomposite was almost constant at higher intensity in the pH range 2–5 and then gradually decreased up to pH 10. This probably was ascribed to the conjugation between CQDs and PANI, followed by a dramatic decrease in the fluorescence intensity at higher pH which facilitates the energy transfer between CQDs and PANI (Fig. 5) [81]. Thus, CQDs/PANI nanocomposite can be applied for studying its sensing performances towards PA in slightly acidic and neutral aqueous media. Zeta potential measurements also demonstrated that the nanoparticles are negatively charged, with a Zeta potential of −10.5 mV. However, the Zeta potential of nanocomposite reached +9.3 mV.

For the comparison between pristine CQDs and CQDs/PANI nanocomposite, the fluorescence measurements were applied at pH 5. The detailed fluorescence of CQDs/PANI nanocomposite after the functionalization of polyaniline with 1 mmol of aniline (Fig. 6 a) was investigated and found to be excitation dependent showing different emissions with the highest intensity at 320 nm. The fluorescence intensity of the CQDs/PANI nanocomposite was shifted to 1016 compared to pristine CQDs (1803) at excitation wavelength 360 nm (Fig. 6b). This might be due to the presence of PANI hosting the CQDs and decreasing electron delocalization densities of CQDs, resulting in a much lower fluorescence intensity.

The fluorescence measurements of CQDs and nanocomposite were utilized for sensing PA in the range 0.37–4.28 μM by adjusting the pH at 5 (Fig. 7 ). It was found that the fluorescence intensity of pristine CQDs quite decreased with a low rate in the presence of PA as its concentration increased (Fig. 7a). On the contrary, the PL emission of CQDs/PANI nanocomposite decreased dramatically with the increase of PA concentration as explained in Fig. 7b. The Stern–Volmer plots (Fig. 7c, d) represent the linear relationship of PA concentration effect on the fluorescence intensity with a limit of detection (LOD) 0.23 and 0.056 μM for pristine CQDs and CQDs/PANI nanocomposite, respectively. The quenching fluorescence was further studied onto CQDs/An solution before polymerization and LOD was found to be 0.19 μM (Fig. S6). Clearly, CQDs/PANI nanocomposite exhibits a higher sensitivity to PA than pristine CQDs.

On the other hand, the CQDs/PANI sensor was further used for PA detection at much lower concentrations (<4 μM) in the range 0.008–0.75 μM. The LOD reached 20 nM (Fig. S7) with a quenching sensitivity (Ksv ) up to 0.345 × 106 M−1 as shown in Table S1. This proves that CQDs/PANI nanocomposite can be used as a sensor probe for PA detection compared with pristine CQDs even at low diluted concentrations, whereas CQDs could not detect PA in the range 0.008–0.75 μM. The validation parameters of the formed nanocomposite for the detection of PA are given in Table 1 . Considering the above insights on the sensing uptake of PA, the current synthesized nanocomposite shows a distinguished sensitivity compared to previously reported structurally designed materials as shown in Table 2 .

The quenching process could occur by a photoexcitation of electrons to the conduction band under fluorescence that may create e-h+ pairs, where two possible mechanisms may take place: (1) some electrons could be trapped by its surface defects preventing the recombination of e- h+ pairs and (2) some electrons could be captured by dissolved oxygen generating superoxide radicals and the h+ might react with surface molecules forming OH• (hydroxyl) radicals [87,88]. Moreover, PA is known to be a highly electron deficient where its presence make the excited electrons to be transferred to HOMO of PA which emits then a non-radiative decay to the ground state and thus the fluorescence is significantly quenched [86].

The mechanism of interaction might have occurred between the fluorophore and quencher molecules in the ground state. On the other hand, the acidic hydroxyl group of PA plays an important role in the interaction of PA with CQDs/PANI nanocomposite. Aniline polymerization in an aqueous acidic medium yields the most conductive form of PANI; the emeraldine salt (ES) as shown in Fig. S8.

Polyaniline with positive charge, due to the available protonated imine groups on the surface of PANI salt, can interact with the hydroxyl group of PA, which results in an electrostatic interaction as previously reported by Wu et al. [75]. They investigated the detection of PA in aqueous water by using water-soluble amine-capped CQDs and indicated strong electrostatic interaction between the anion–cation pair of PA and NH2 +. Therefore, as seen in the obtained results and previous reports, it can be concluded that one of the main reasons of the high sensitivity of CQDs/PANI nanocomposite towards PA is the interaction between OH group of PA and protonated PANI-ES in CQDs/PANI nanocomposite through electrostatic interactions. Also, the π-π charge transfer interactions between CQDs/PANI nanocomposite and PA can also be included.

The mechanism of interaction can be further investigated by the significant overlap between the emission spectrum of CQDs/PANI nanocomposite and the absorption spectrum of PA as shown in Fig. 8 a. Hence, a possibility of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE) mechanisms may occur where both can enhance the sensing detection [89]. According to measuring the lifetime decay for the nanocomposite in the presence and absence of picric acid, it was found that the average lifetime changed from 2.5 ns (CQDs/PANI nanocomposite) to 3.3 ns in the presence of PA. The changes in lifetime occurred beside the spectral overlap introduced the quenching through FRET mechanism. When measuring the absorbance of PA (inset Fig. 8b), it was found that its absorbance appears at 356 nm with a small shoulder at 402 nm. Consequently, upon adding PA to CQDs/PANI nanocomposite (Fig. 8b), a new peak appeared at the same wavelength that corresponds to PA band, implying the successful interaction between CQDs/PANI nanocomposite and quencher (PA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study the sensor nanocomposite selectivity and real sample detections
ce:section.ce:para: The current synthesized CQDs/PANI nanocomposite exhibits enhanced sensing performances in terms of sensitivity and selectivity towards PA with the interference of other nitro and aromatic compounds (Fig. S9), such as ammonium nitrate, BA, B, PNP, Res, 3-AP, 4-AP, Tol, NB, and o-ClP. The fluorescence measurements were obtained at pH 5 of concentration 4 μM. Obviously, Fig. 9 a represents the emission of different compounds in the presence and absence of PA. It was found that the quenching increases after the addition of PA to a mixture containing other interferences, demonstrating the distinguished selectivity of CQDs/PANI towards PA. In addition, the sensitivity and selectivity of nanocomposite was further measured for PA beside different metal ions such as Co2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Pd2+, Pt4+, Ca2+, Cu2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Sn2+ and Zn2+ (Fig. 9b). Interestingly, the CQDs/PANI nanocomposite showed a lower fluorescence for other aromatic, nitro compounds, and metal ions in comparison to PA that exhibits dramatic quenching in the fluorescence intensity. These results reveal that the synthesized nanocomposite shows a distinguished sensing performance for PA at diluted concentrations.

To check the ability of CQDs/PANI nanocomposite to detect PA for realistic applications, the CQDs capped with PANI was tested in spiked water. Tap water samples were spiked with different concentrations of PA and a recovery procedure was applied with triplicate runs (n = 3). The PA recovery (R %) from water by CQDs/PANI nanocomposite was estimated from Eq. (5). Table 3 shows different amounts of PA spiked to tap water with a recovery measured
(5)
R % =
Found
Spiked
× 100

It was found that R% was highly acceptable in the range 91–104%. In addition, the relative standard deviation (RSD %) was found below <5% which indicates its good sensitivity and high feasibility towards PA in the real samples.

In conclusion, the in-situ polymerization of aniline wrapped CQDs to synthesize CQDs/PANI nanocomposite with an average size of 4 nm was introduced. The in-situ polymerization of aniline with different concentrations, greatly, enhances the quenching luminescence efficiency of CQDs due to the FRET derived from CQDs excited states to PANI π-system. The CQDs/PANI nanocomposite exhibits a distinguished detection sensitivity for PA in slightly acidic and aqueous media at diluted concentrations 0.37–1.42 μM with limit of detection reaching 0.056 μM. The quenching occurred due to the electrostatic interaction between OH group of PA and PANI-ES in the formed nanocomposite with a possibility of π-π charge transfer interaction. The nanocomposite remarkably enhanced the sensing performance of PA in terms of selectivity at room temperature over other aromatic, nitro compounds, and distinct metal ions in spiked tap water. The results showed that both the average recoveries and relative standard deviations in real samples could meet practical requirements.
